observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to require the:
(i) Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant at the beginning of the year, a certified list of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year for the establishment of an appropriate and correct Real Property and Special Education Taxes Receivables based on the Real Property Account Register/Taxpayer’s Index Card;
(ii) Municipal Accountant to: (a) regularly take up in the books of accounts the total RPT/SET tax dues based on the certified list of tax due and collectible furnished by the Municipal Treasurer; (b) coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer for proper reconciliation of the discrepancy in the recorded balances of RPT and SET Receivables as against the total amount of tax delinquencies indicated in the Real Property Tax Owners Delinquency Listing and prepare the necessary adjusting Journal Entry Voucher to correct the balances of RPT and SET Receivable accounts in the financial statements as at year-end; and (c) ensure that RPT and SET Receivables are properly recognized only in the year they were earned and advance payments are reclassified to income in the year to which they pertain; and
(iii) Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to analyse and determine the cause of the discrepancies between their records, prepare reconciliation and make the necessary adjustments, and submit to this Office the actions taken to establish the correct balances of the subject receivables.

Not Implemented
(ii)(d)/(iii) The reconciliation process between MAccO and MTO is ongoing. The Municipal Accountant and Treasurer are to analyse and determine causes of discrepancies and submit reconciliation and adjustments. observations.reason:
The Municipal Accountant was still not furnished at the beginning of year with the certified list of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for CY 2024 since the process of computing RPT is done manually by the Office of the Municipal Treasurer, which usually takes time hence, the delay in the set-up of RPT and SET Receivables. Similar audit observation was noted in CY 2024 transactions as reiterated in Part II of this report.

The Municipal Accountant had begun implementing the recommended accounting entries and drew several Journal Entries for adjustment and reclassification. However, some entries were based on previous year amounts and further adjustments were required.

Reconciliation between the Accounting Office and MTO records was not made at year-end; reconciliation was made only in January of the following year. The Municipal Treasurer continues to exert efforts, but collections and discrepancies persist. Some adjustments were made but the exact over/understatement could not be computed due to voluminous prior years’ transactions and scope limitation.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require/instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to (i) prioritize the reconciliation of the deposits in transit, and henceforth ascertain that deposits are recorded in the books at the time these are actually deposited to the AGDB; (ii) prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to account for the stale checks for fair and accurate presentation of the Cash in Bank – LCCA and the related Payable account in the Statement of Financial Position as at year-end; and (ii) analyze all reconciling items identified in the BRS and prepare the corresponding JEVs to ensure that the Cash in Bank account reflects the correct balance; and
b. Municipal Treasurer to (i) send written notices to the payees one month before the check becomes stale; (ii) for stale checks already issued to the payees, immediately inform them to return the same to the Municipal Treasurer’s Office, for cancellation and issuance of the necessary check replacements thereof; (iii) for stale checks which have been unclaimed, immediately cancel the same and furnish the Municipal Accountant with the related original DVs and supporting documents, to facilitate adjustment in the books; and (iv) henceforth ensure that all checks issued are properly monitored, to avoid accumulation of stale checks.

In view of the foregoing, we reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that the Municipal Mayor ensure strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances to avoid accumulation of outstanding cash advances.

In addition, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the
(a) Municipal Treasurer to exhaust all the possible measures to recover and collect the dormant receivable accounts within the bounds of law prior to initiating any request for write-off and/or disposition; and (b) Municipal Accountant to conduct a (i) comprehensive evaluation of all dormant receivables, particularly those non-moving for 10 years or more and effect proper disposition thereof, as warranted, in accordance with prescribed procedures in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016, as amended by COA Circular No. 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023; (ii) detailed collectability assessment of dormant Due from Officers and Employees account prior to recognizing any allowance for impairment in accordance with IPSAS 29; and (iii) prepare the necessary Journal Entry Voucher to reclassify the affected accounts for the fair presentation of the Financial Statements.

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer ensure the timely renewal of fidelity bonds of AOs and that cash advances granted to AOs are within their maximum cash accountability.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
 a. GSO and Inventory Committee to consistently conduct a complete physical count of all its Inventories and PPEs of the Municipality, prepare and submit the corresponding RPCI and RPCPPE;
 b. Municipal Accountant and GSO to maintain perpetual inventory records to facilitate proper reconciliation of Inventories and PPEs; and
 c. GSO to facilitate disposal of all unserviceable properties of the Municipality.

We also reiterated that the Municipal Mayor submit the proofs of distribution/issuance of the Agricultural and Marine Supplies for Distribution amounting to P1,485,331.00 to the Accounting Unit for the preparation of the corresponding JEV.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
(a) Municipal Engineer to closely monitor the rectification of the identified deficiencies and ensure that the contractor complies with the required corrective measures; and henceforth ensure that projects funded out of grants from NGAs are completed in accordance with the approved plans within the target date of completion; and
(b) Municipal Accountant to (i) expedite the validation of the amounts Due to NGAs and facilitate the immediate remittance thereof; (ii) effect the necessary adjustments; and (iii) conduct regular monitoring of the status on the individual subsidiary ledger accounts composing the Due to NGAs account to avoid misrepresentation of the accounts in the financial statements; and (iii) cause the return of the unexpended balances of completed PPAs and amounts due for remittance to other NGAs for proper presentation of the Due to NGAs account in the Financial Statements and/or make representations with the source agency for the possible reprogramming of the unexpended balances to other PPAs.

We also recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the project implementers/end-users to fast track the utilization of funds granted by various SAs to ensure that benefits from the PPAs are immediately realized by the constituents.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to conduct a comprehensive evaluation of all the Inter-Agency Payables and exert effort to retrieve the documents of those without details to properly classify and adjust the amounts recorded for the fair presentation of the Statement of Financial Position as at year-end.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to conduct a comprehensive evaluation of the Other Payables account and exert effort to retrieve the documents of those without details to properly classify and adjust the amounts recorded for the fair presentation of the Statement of Financial Position as at year-end.

We reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the (i) Barangay to submit complete and appropriate liquidation documents set under COA Circular Nos. 2012-001 before recognizing fund transfers as liquidated. Require the Barangay to submit the original sales invoices and other necessary documents to support their fund liquidation; and (ii) Municipal Accountant to adjust the books of accounts, if necessary, to reflect the correct status of unliquidated fund transfers.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the:
(i) Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant at the beginning of the year, a certified list of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year for the establishment of an appropriate and correct Real Property and Special Education Taxes Receivables based on the Real Property Account Register/Taxpayer’s Index Card;
(ii) Municipal Accountant to: (a) regularly take up in the books of accounts the total RPT/SET tax dues based on the certified list of tax due and collectible furnished by the Municipal Treasurer; (b) coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer for proper reconciliation of the discrepancy in the recorded balances of RPT and SET Receivables as against the total amount of tax delinquencies indicated in the Real Property Tax Owners Delinquency Listing and prepare the necessary adjusting Journal Entry Voucher to correct the balances of RPT and SET Receivable accounts in the financial statements as at year-end; and (c) ensure that RPT and SET Receivables are properly recognized only in the year they were earned and advance payments are reclassified to income in the year to which they pertain; and
(iii) Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to analyse and determine the cause of the discrepancies between their records, prepare reconciliation and make the necessary adjustments, and submit to this Office the actions taken to establish the correct balances of the subject receivables.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to (i) exert more effort and undertake effective, efficient and aggressive tax collection strategies to improve collection by conducting massive tax campaigns and consistent preparation and issuance of Notices of Delinquency for all delinquent Real Property Units; (ii) monitor the payment of real property taxes due for the current year and impose the available remedies and procedures as authorized under the Local Government Code; (iii) recommend to the Provincial Treasurer, for the delinquent real property taxes with NDs already served, the imposition of the legal remedies either by administrative auction thru levy on real property or judicial action, following the procedures authorized in the LGC, to enforce collection and to undertake the same expeditiously on a yearly basis to reduce amounts accumulated as receivables for delinquent taxes; and (iv) conduct dialogue with owners different from the records on file to address concerns on the collection of taxes. If warranted, request the presence of the Provincial Treasurer and Provincial Legal Officer to assist in such undertaking.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer for proper reconciliation of the discrepancy in the recorded balances of RPT and SET Receivables as against the Report of Collections by the MTO and prepare the necessary adjustments to present the correct and reliable balances of the RPT and SET Receivable accounts.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together with the Municipal Budget Officer, and the rest of the Municipal Development Council, ensure strict compliance with the provisions of Section 3.2.5 of the DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1, on the expenditures not allowed to be charged against the 20 percent DF, to ensure optimal funds for development projects.

We also reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Heads of the Municipal Offices to review the DTRs and Accomplishment Reports of the contractual personnel/Job Order contract under their respective offices before approving the same to ensure that pertinent supporting documents were duly accomplished and signed by the employees concerned; and
b. Human Resource Management Officer to carefully monitor and check the actual services rendered in the DTRs and reconcile the same with the payrolls, to avoid overpayments.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor, through the LDDRM Council: (i) ensure the optimal utilization of the LDRRMF, including the unexpended balances from previous years, to enhance the Municipality’s resilience and capacity to reduce vulnerabilities when exposed to hazards and calamities; and (ii) see to it that LDRRM Funds are used solely for disaster risk reduction and management activities pursuant to the NDRRMC, DBM and DILG JMC No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor, with the assistance of the Local Development Council, Municipal Planning and Development Officer and the Municipal Engineer to maximize the utilization of the 20% DF to spur development, provide jobs to constituents, and achieve the desired socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the LGU for the intended users/beneficiaries to enjoy the immediate benefits from said development projects.

The Management has yet to provide its written comments and/or justifications on the possible reprogramming of unexpended balances and/or refund of the said balances and delay in the implementation of PPAs.

However, collections are still not as anticipated due to factors such as unknown whereabouts of some landowners, multiple owners/mother titles, and lack of full exercise of legal remedies by the Provincial Government. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together with the Municipal Budget Officer, and the rest of the Municipal Development Council, ensure strict compliance with the provisions of Section 3.2.5 of the DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1, on the expenditures not allowed to be charged against the 20 percent DF.

We also reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

observations.managementAction:
Implemented
The Management took note of the audit recommendations and applied the same in succeeding transactions. Several lacking supporting documents were still not submitted to the Audit Team because they prioritized the implementation of the audit recommendations in CY 2024 transactions. With regard to the overpayments, the concerned officials agreed to deduct the overpayments to the succeeding amount due from the concerned personnel. In addition, the Management agreed with the observations that DTRs were prepared by only one or two persons for the reason that the group or Job-Order personnel belong to the indigenous people (IP) community and are incapacitated to sign/prepare the payrolls/DTRs.
observations.reason: The Management implemented procedures and monitored DTRs and ARs; however, some payrolls remained supported with incomplete documentation and inconsistencies. Some supporting documents were not submitted to the Audit Team as they prioritized corrective actions in subsequent transactions. Overpayments were agreed to be deducted from succeeding payrolls.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to require the:
a. Concerned contractor to correct and rectify the aforementioned deficiency at his/her own expense, and provide pictures and/or proofs of compliance to the Municipal Accountant for the eventual submission to the Audit Team. Otherwise, require the refund of the equivalent cost of the defect/deficiency; and
b. Municipal Engineer to closely monitor the project implementation and ensure that projects are implemented in accordance with the approved plans and specifications.
observations.managementAction:

Implemented

observations.reason: The contractor refunded/deduction was effected from the contractor's billing and the Municipal Engineer committed to institute improved monitoring and to require the contractor to submit a detailed "catch-up" program to eliminate slippages. Similar deficiencies were observed in CY 2024 transactions and reiterated in Part II.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. BAC and its TWG to: (i) be cautious and exercise prudence in the examination of eligibility and technical documents submitted by the prospective bidders in faithful adherence to relevant provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to ensure that the contract will be awarded only to eligible contractors/suppliers; (ii) immediately submit the lacking bidding documents to the Audit Team to arrive at a decision in audit; (iii) ensure strict compliance with the provisions set forth in Rule No. 36 of the IRR of Republic Act No. 9502 and EO No. 49 regarding the procurement of drugs and medicines; and (iv) strictly comply with the prescribed period of furnishing the COA Auditor copies of contracts including the documentary requirements to facilitate timely review and evaluation as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012;
b. Municipal Engineer to prepare the ABC in the format prescribed under DPWH Department Order No. 197 s. 2016; and
c. Municipal Accountant to: (i) refrain from booking-up as CIP accounts the programmed cost of the projects yet to be implemented in consonance with the definition of the said account as set forth in Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009; (ii) immediately submit the lacking supporting documents in the claims for payment, and henceforth ensure that all claims/payments are supported with complete and proper documents before processing for payment; and (iii) ensure the correctness and accuracy of the computation of liquidated damages for delayed projects.
observations.managementAction:
Implemented
The BAC Secretariat submitted explanations and justifications together with several lacking supporting documents via letter dated June 6, 2025 and assured compliance. The Municipal Engineer adjusted preparation of ABCs following prescribed formats and committed to be vigilant in future. The Municipal Accountant prepared required journal entries and submitted some lacking documents. Some procurements and payments had incomplete supporting documents which remained to be submitted for validation. Retention money collection and liquidated damages were effected where applicable.
observations.reason: The Management took note of the audit recommendations and submitted some lacking documents; however, several lacking supporting documents were still not submitted to the Audit Team as these were misplaced or prioritized for corrective action in subsequent transactions. Some deficiencies persisted in CY 2024 transactions as reiterated in Part II.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing, we recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to comply with the existing provisions in regard with realignment or reprogramming of PPAs.

We further recommended and the Municipal Mayor also agreed to direct the Municipal Budget Officer to refrain from charging prior year expenses to current year appropriation to present proper matching of income and expenses.
observations.managementAction:
Implemented
Realignment/reprogramming of PPAs are already supported with Appropriation Ordinance. The Municipal Budget Officer refrained from charging prior year expenses to current year appropriation; obligations for current year appropriation were charged and paid during the year.

The MDRRMO acknowledged errors and explained some expenditures were for IEC campaign and disaster operations. They committed that similar errors will not occur in the future.
observations.reason: Some realignments/reprogramming actions were supported with Appropriation Ordinance. Proofs of compliance were not submitted for all adjustments; several actions were effected only on transactions approved and paid after last year’s exit conference. Some expenditures charged against LDRRMF were acknowledged as oversight and will be prevented in future.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, with the assistance of the Local Development Council, the Municipal Planning and Development Officer and the Municipal Engineer: (i) judiciously plan and evaluate target PPAs to facilitate effective and efficient implementation thereof for the benefit of the Municipality; and (ii) to maximize the utilization of the 20% Development Fund to spur development, provide jobs to constituents and to achieve the desired socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the LGU so that the intended users/beneficiaries may enjoy the immediate benefits from said development projects.
observations.managementAction:
Implemented
Some PPAs were prioritized and implemented; several unimplemented PPAs remained due to reasons such as unavailability of materials, need for MOAs with implementing partners, requirement for ordinance approval for realignments, insufficient funding, and pending procurement/technical preparations. The Municipal Engineer commented several projects were already implemented during the 1st semester of CY 2025.

The Municipal Accountant and MPDO coordinated on implementation and realignment where applicable.
observations.reason: Of the CY 2024 appropriations amounting to P59,088,316.51 (current and continuing), P40,437,588.50 was expended during the year registering an overall financial delivery rate of 68.44 percent. Several PPAs were not implemented due to procurement delays, pending MOAs (e.g., with UPLB, NCIP), late approvals, technical specifications, insufficient funds and timing issues. This resulted in unimplemented PPAs as listed and reiterated in Part II.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the GAD Focal Point System: (i) ensure that funds allocated for GAD programs and activities are at least 5% of the Municipality’s Annual Budget pursuant to Item 4.C.1.1 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01; and (ii) ascertain that the GPB and accomplishment reports are submitted to the Audit Team within five (5) working days from receipt of the  reviewed and endorsed plan from the DILG pursuant to Item V of COA Circular No. 2014-001, to properly evaluate and assess whether GAD PPAs embodied in the GPB that are relevant and responsive to gender issues and concerns of the Municipality are properly implemented.
observations.reason: The Management still failed to comply with the audit recommendations hence, similar audit observations had been observed in CY 2021 transactions as discussed in Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure strict compliance with the pertinent laws, rules and regulations on the monitoring, utilization of COVID-19 funds. Likewise, the Municipal Mayor is advised to require the:
Municipal Accountant to: (i) immediately submit the required supporting documents, enumerated in the preceding paragraphs, to the Audit Team to be able to arrive at a decision in audit; (ii) ensure that claims are duly supported with complete and proper documentation before processing of payment; and (iii) ensure that accounting forms are duly accomplished; and
Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to ensure that all procurements for COVID-19 related supplies and equipment are made strictly in accordance with the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: (a) Inventory Committee  to conduct physical count for both Inventories and PPEs of the Municipality, prepare and submit the corresponding duly accomplished RPCI and RPCPPE, and henceforth, ensure that said physical count are strictly and promptly conducted semestrally and annually for Inventories and PPEs, respectively;                 (b) Municipal Accountant and the OIC-GSO to maintain perpetual inventory records, with all the required information, which shall be kept updated and reconciled with the book balances at all times. Discrepancies, if any, shall be properly examined and verified to facilitate adjustments of the affected accounts and pinpoint accountabilities therefore; (c) Municipal Accountant, upon reconciliation, to prepare the necessary adjustments on the affected accounts for fair and accurate presentation in the Statement of Financial Position; and (d) OIC-GSO to prepare and submit duly accomplished Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP), together with the required supporting documents to facilitate the disposal of unserviceable items.

In view thereof, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) ensure strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular 97-002 on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances to avoid accumulation of outstanding cash advances;       (b) make a formal request for the writing-off, together with the necessary supporting documents, of unliquidated cash advances of those who are already deceased and of fund transfer to Barangay without supporting documents, which have been dormant for more than 10 years, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005; and (c) enforce full settlement of the outstanding undeposited collection of one accountable officer and the liability of the former Municipal Mayor over the lost Municipal vehicle in accordance with the approved installment payment scheme.
We also recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the:
Municipal Accountant to: (i) exhaust all the possible remedies to collect the receivables and make formal demand to the concerned AOs for the settlement/liquidation of cash advances and fund transfers; (ii) strictly monitor the grant of cash advance by issuing certification that previous cash advances have been liquidated and accounted for in the books to prevent the grant of additional cash advances to those employees and officers with outstanding cash advances; (iii) practice prudence in the grant of cash advances to casual/job order employees whose connection and tenure with the Municipality is uncertain, there being no employee-employer relationship; and (iv) ensure that the grant and liquidation of cash advances are duly supported with proper and complete supporting documents, such as ObRs, to establish the validity, propriety and legality of the transactions; and
Municipal Budget Officer to duly prepare ObRs for all transactions including cash advances to ensure that valid and legal expenditures and obligations are taken up in the accounts of the current fiscal year pursuant to Section 119 of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the LDRRM Council,      (i) ensure that at least five percent of the total estimated revenue from regular resources is allocated for disaster risk reduction and management activities; and (ii) see to it that LDRRM Funds are used solely for disaster risk reduction and management activities pursuant to the provisions of R.A. No. 10121.
We further recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal Accountant to transfer the CY 2021 unexpended appropriation for Capital Outlay (CO) to the General Fund, and henceforth ensure that any unappropriated balances for CO shall remain in the General Fund Books until the project funded therefore is completed.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, with the assistance of the Local Development Council, the Municipal Planning and Development Officer and the Municipal Engineer: i) maximize the utilization of the 20 percent Development Fund to spur development, provide jobs to constituents and to achieve the desired socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the LGU so that the intended users/beneficiaries may enjoy the immediate benefits from said development projects; and ii) ensure that all expenditures charged against the Funds are allowed under DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1, dated November 4, 2020.

Municipal Treasurer to ensure that payments are made only on duly approved disbursement vouchers and require written authorization from the supplier/contractor for claimants other than the payee.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to prioritize the prompt submission of financial reports and documents to the Audit Team to facilitate timely review and audit of financial transactions so that deficiencies that may be found in audit can be communicated early to concerned officials and repetitions of the same can be avoided in succeeding transactions.

In view thereof, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the GAD Focal Point System: (i) ensure that funds allocated for GAD programs and activities are at least 5% of the Municipality’s Annual Budget pursuant to Item 4.C.1.1 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01; (ii) see to it that revisions and/or changes in the approved GPB are submitted to the PPDO and DILG for review and endorsement; and (iii) ascertain that the GPB and accomplishment reports are submitted to the Audit Team within five (5) working days from receipt of the  reviewed and endorsed plan from the DILG pursuant to Item V of COA Circular No. 2014-001 to properly evaluate and assess whether GAD PPAs embodied therein are relevant and responsive to gender issues and concerns of the Municipality.

We recommended for the settlement of audit suspension within 90 days from receipt of the Notice of Suspension, pursuant to Section 9.4 of the Rules and Regulations on Settlement of Accounts. The head of the agency is responsible for ensuring that the requirements of transactions suspended in audit are complied with.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the: (a) Inventory Committee to ensure that the RPCPPE are based on the results of actual physical count so that any unrecorded PPE found on location and/or losses discovered during the physical count could be immediately investigated and adjusted in the books of accounts; and (b) Municipal Health Officer to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant with regards to the medicines received from the DOH.  Account for the quantity and value of the medicines and prepare the inventory report thereof for the Municipal Accountant to record the same in the books of accounts.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to: (a) prepare the necessary adjustments on the balance of the Motor Vehicle account for the stolen service vehicle amounting to P1,105,000.00 and the down payment of P142,000.00 previously recorded as an expense;           (b) reclassify the stolen service vehicle amounting to P1,238,500.00 to the Due from Officers and Employees account as a personal liability of Hon. Lily R. Estoya; (c) coordinate with the Municipal Health Officer for the proper inventory and reconciliation of the drugs and medicines on hand as of December 31, 2018 and prepare the necessary adjustments for the correct presentation of the related inventory and expense accounts; and (d) ensure that all goods and supplies acquired and/or received from other government agencies be properly recorded and monitored in the books of accounts under the related Inventory account and expensed when used and/or issued for fair and accurate presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.

observations.recommendation:

Utilizations of the Fund were still made not in strict compliance with the NDRRMC- DBM-DILG guidelines.

The foregoing are discussed in the Part II of this Report.

We also recommended to desist from using the 20% DF for non-priority expenditures such as salaries and wages, purchase of office equipment, meals and snacks, travelling expenses and repairs and maintenance pursuant to Section 4.0 of DILG-DBM JMC 2017-001.

We recommended for the optimal utilization of the LDRRM Fund to enhance the resilience of the constituents should disaster and calamities arise in the future. We also recommended that the LDRRM Council decide on the use of the unexpended balance of previous years’ LDRRMF which shall be incorporated in the LDRRMF Investment Plan.
We further recommended that the unexpended balance of the LDRRM Fund be transferred to Trust Fund books under Trust Liability – DRRM account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, with the help of the Municipal Accountant, exert efforts to enforce the settlement of suspensions and disallowances by sending letters to the concerned persons responsible/liable requiring them to settle their accountabilities.

We recommended that the OIC – Municipal Accountant and the OIC – General Services Officer maintain updated PPELC and property cards, respectively, to facilitate reconciliation with the RPCPPE. Any discrepancy between these records should be properly examined to facilitate adjustment of the affected PPE account balances for its fair presentation in the Statement of Financial Position. Ensure that records are supplied with relevant information to easily identify and locate PPE in the next conduct of physical count of all agency’s property. 

We also recommended to include in the conduct of physical inventory all the local roads and other infrastructure assets under the Municipality’s jurisdictions and reconcile inventory report with those recorded in the Registry of Public Infrastructure. 

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct concerned officials to conduct test for impairment of PPE totalling P10,821,022.11 for its proper valuation in the Statement of Financial Position and if confirmed that the carrying amount of these property exceeded the recoverable service amount, an impairment loss should be recognized in the books of accounts. We also recommended for the disposal of unserviceable PPE.
